definitions.definition: a form of this process used in some cooking devices, where metal coils (= wires) in the device create a magnetic field that induces (= causes) an electric current in the metal base of a pan, creating heat
examples: Make sure that the pan is removed only when the food is cooked, otherwise the induction of heat will stop.

In induction cooking the energy is transferred directly to the pan by electro-magnetic field heating, so very little heat is wasted.

The large mullioned window inset with heraldic glass appears to be early 16th century.

definitions.definition: to position something within a larger object
examples: Small lights were inset into the side of the stair treads.
When the lens is inset inside the fixture, the light is directed where it is needed.

definitions.definition: something positioned within a larger object
examples: The map has an inset (= a small extra map) in the top corner that shows the city centre in more detail.
The long black wool coat has gold satin insets along its back and sleeves.

definitions.definition: near or towards the coast
examples: an inshore fishing zone
an inshore lifeboat
The ships moved slowly inshore.

definitions.definition: the part, space, or side of something that is inside
examples: Did you clean the inside of the car?
The hotel looked shabby from the street, but it was fine on the inside.
the insides of people's houses

definitions.definition: someone's mind, thoughts, or feelings; someone's private self
examples: Nobody knows how I really feel on the inside.

definitions.definition: The inside of a part of the body such as the arm or leg is the part facing in towards the rest of the body
examples: She dabbed perfume on the inside of her wrist.

definitions.definition: a person's or animal's internal organs, especially the stomach or bowels
examples: The dead seal's insides were spread all over the snow.

definitions.definition: in or into a room, building, container, or something similar
examples: "Is Anna in the garden?" "No, she's inside (= in the house)."
She grabbed the bag and looked inside.

definitions.definition: in someone's mind, thoughts, or feelings
examples: She couldn't cope with the grief she felt inside.
He was outwardly calm, but inside he was in turmoil.

definitions.definition: in prison
examples: Her husband's inside for armed robbery.

definitions.definition: If something is inside out, it has the usual inside part on the outside and the usual outside part on the inside
examples: She had her sweater on inside out.

definitions.definition: in a room, building, container, or something similar
examples: What's inside the box?
Luckily, no one was inside the building when it collapsed.

definitions.definition: in someone's mind, thoughts, or feelings
examples: Who can tell what goes on inside his head?
Rage was building up inside her.

definitions.definition: within the limits of what is allowed by something, for example the law or a set of rules
examples: Investment banks are struggling to find a way of doing business that stays inside the rules.
We have to work from inside the system to improve outcomes.

definitions.definition: on or in the inner part of something such as a container or building
examples: He put the documents carefully in his inside pocket.

definitions.definition: (of information) obtained by someone in a group, organization, or company and therefore involving special or secret knowledge
examples: inside information/knowledge
I'll call up Clare and get the inside story (= a true report of the facts).

definitions.definition: someone who is an accepted member of a group and who therefore has special or secret knowledge or influence
examples: According to insiders, the committee is having difficulty making up its mind.

definitions.definition: (of something unpleasant or dangerous) gradually and secretly causing harm
examples: High blood pressure is an insidious condition which has few symptoms.

definitions.definition: (of something unpleasant or dangerous) in a way that gradually and secretly causes harm
examples: The disease begins insidiously and progresses relentlessly.
This has quietly and insidiously grown into a big problem.

definitions.definition: the quality of gradually and secretly causing harm
examples: We must recognise the insidiousness of these racial stereotypes.
The insidiousness of this disease is that you don't know who has it.

definitions.definition: (the ability to have) a clear, deep, and sometimes sudden understanding of a complicated problem or situation
examples: It was an interesting book, full of fascinating insights into human relationships.

definitions.definition: showing a clear and usually original understanding of a complicated problem or situation
examples: She has written an insightful account of the modern art world.
He was, of course, very funny and insightful.

definitions.definition: an object or mark that shows that a person belongs to a particular organization or group, or has a particular rank
examples: the royal insignia

definitions.definition: the fact of being small or not noticeable, and therefore not considered important
examples: Advances in astronomy have reinforced the utter insignificance of Earth on a celestial scale.
The traumas of my own upbringing pale/fade into insignificance (= seem very unimportant) when I hear stories about the way Peter's parents treated him.

definitions.definition: small or not noticeable, and therefore not considered important
examples: Why bother arguing about such an insignificant amount of money?
The difference between the two results was insignificant.

definitions.definition: in a way that is small or not noticeable, and therefore not considered important
examples: Health officials dismiss the problem as insignificantly small.
I am being punished, and not insignificantly.

definitions.definition: pretending to feel something that you do not really feel, or not meaning what you say
examples: an insincere apology
And all this praise just because the poor man has died - doesn't it strike you as a bit insincere?

definitions.definition: in a way that shows that you are pretending to feel something that you do not really feel, or do not mean what you say
examples: She smiled insincerely at Howard from time to time.
He sent his love insincerely to Kate and hung up.

definitions.definition: the action or practice of pretending to feel something that you do not really feel, or not meaning what you say
examples: There’s often a bit of insincerity in these speeches.

definitions.definition: to suggest, without being direct, that something unpleasant is true

definitions.definition: not having a strong taste or character, or having no interest or energy

definitions.definition: the quality of not having a strong taste or character, or of lacking interest or energy

definitions.definition: in a way that does not have a strong taste or character, or lacks interest or energy

definitions.definition: to say firmly or demand forcefully, especially when others disagree with or oppose what you say

Please go first - I insist!

definitions.definition: to look at something or someone carefully in order to discover information, especially about their quality or condition

examples: The golden autumn light provided the inspiration for the painting.

definitions.definition: a sudden good idea
examples: He had an inspiration - why not apply for some government money?

definitions.definition: a meeting at which someone with a drug or alcohol problem is asked by family members, friends, or health workers to accept the fact that they have a problem and is encouraged to get treatment
